title: Plate Tectonics
puzzle: Why does Earth’s surface look stable even though it is always moving?
insight: Earth’s crust is broken into huge plates that drift slowly over the hotter mantle below, reshaping continents, mountains, and oceans over immense timescales.
key_ideas: Plate motion creates earthquakes and volcanoes.
Continents have moved dramatically through deep time.
Surface landscapes are shaped by slow, persistent motion.
mind_blower: The ground beneath your feet is part of a restless planetary skin, not a permanent platform.
next_curiosity: If continents drift so slowly, how did scientists become sure they were moving at all?
narration_text: Why does Earth’s surface look stable even though it is always moving? Earth’s crust is broken into huge plates that drift slowly over the hotter mantle below, reshaping continents, mountains, and oceans over immense timescales. Plate motion creates earthquakes and volcanoes. Continents have moved dramatically through deep time. Surface landscapes are shaped by slow, persistent motion. The ground beneath your feet is part of a restless planetary skin, not a permanent platform. If continents drift so slowly, how did scientists become sure they were moving at all?
